data.title: Turning Point Brands: 2 Minor Reasons For Major Sell-Off
data.description:
Turning Point Brands, Inc. (TPB) reported solid Q4 results, but the stock still ended up plummeting by -21% post-earnings. The nicotine product company’s nicotine pouch momentum is guided to slow down, and marketing expenses continue to weigh on profitability, not supported by the stock’s previously overly enthusiastic valuation. Yet, given TPB’s conservative guiding approach, I believe that the report isn’t cause for much concern.

I initiated and maintained a Hold rating in my previous May 2025 article on the stock, titled “Turning Point Brands: Alp's Momentum Accelerates, But Upside Is Limited”. The stock has since still returned 44%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 22% gain.

TPB Q4 Review: The Focus Remains on Nicotine Pouches

TPB reported good growth to the end of 2025. The company’s two nicotine pouch brands, Fre and Alp, continue to drive the growth narrative. Other products’ performance has been slower, but they are becoming less critical for the company as TPB is clearly focusing its efforts on the two growth brands. Revenues came in at $121.0 million for Q4, up by 29.2% year-on-year and beating Wall Street’s consensus very slightly.

Growth stayed consistent from previous quarters, again driven by TPB’s nicotine pouch brands or “modern oral” sales in the Stoker’s segment. Modern oral sales reached $41.3 million for Q4 alone, up by 266% year-on-year from quite a low comparison base. Alp’s website traffic has remained high, and Fre has continued to gain momentum from increased distribution in brick-and-mortar stores. TPB is continuing to expand Fre’s distribution to regional and national convenience stores and is now even testing expanding Alp into brick-and-mortar as well. Alp’s brick-and-mortar distribution is expected to scale meaningfully during Q2’2026.

The product category has vast potential beyond 2025. Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) reported an estimated 23% U.S. consumption growth for the leading Zyn brand in Q4; overall industry growth has remained fast, but TPB has mostly achieved growth through market share gains. TPB targets a double-digit market share in nicotine pouches over the long term and believes the category’s overall revenue potential to reach $10 billion by 2030. TPB looks to grow modern oral revenues past $1 billion. Total modern oral sales came in at around $130 million in 2025.

Given TPB’s current nicotine pouch momentum, the brand’s distribution gains, and great category potential, TPB’s 2026 guidance for modern oral sales is clearly disappointing. TPB guides net modern oral revenues at only $180-190 million for 2026. Assuming stable sequential revenue growth throughout the year, TPB would only end up at 19% year-on-year growth for the segment by Q4.

At the same time, TPB’s legacy products have performed quite slowly. Modern oral sales pushed TPB’s Q4 revenues up by around $30.0 million, while the company’s total revenues only increased by $27.3 million; other products’ revenues declined by approximately -$2.7 million. The Zig-Zag segment showed a sharpening -12.8% sales decline to $40.0 million as TPB has chosen to wind down the Clipper business to focus resources on nicotine pouches. Legacy sales in the Stoker’s segment improved by single digits in Q4.

Profitability is also under pressure. TPB has maintained fairly stable gross margins, but the company is investing very heavily in Fre’s and Alp’s marketing efforts; the Stoker’s segment’s operating expenses more than doubled in 2025 to $59.3 million, primarily reflecting marketing expenses for the segment. TPB’s adjusted EBITDA only grew by 14.4% to $30.0 million in Q4. The adjusted EBITDA margin declined by 320 basis points to 24.8%.

Even more notably, TPB assumes adjusted EBITDA to decline to $24-27 million in Q1’2026 from $27.7 million a year ago. Marketing investments are only increasing as TPB is trying to capture share in nicotine pouches, now looking to pressure total earnings.

Ultimately, there seemed to be two key reasons driving a very sharp negative stock reaction: a relatively slow 2026 guidance and increasing profitability pressure. The first seems most likely to have driven such a reaction, in my opinion, but I don’t believe the growth guidance matters as much. TPB initially guided modern oral sales at a $60-80 million range for 2025, but ended up reporting modern oral sales of around $130 million. The 2026 guidance is likely also a very conservative estimate and doesn’t signal a significant slowdown, in my opinion.

A weak EBITDA guidance for Q1 is somewhat concerning, but in my opinion, high marketing expenses are warranted for now as TPB is trying to position Fre and Alp well for the long term. Profitability should rebound over the longer term as revenues scale more in line with marketing expenses.

TPB’s Valuation Is Now Fair

I revised my discounted cash flow (DCF) model estimates for TPB.

I estimate quite similar growth as previously; the 2025-2035 revenue CAGR is now set at 10.8%, but I now even estimate slightly higher 2.5% growth after 2035, as nicotine pouches’ secular growth should boost growth for a very long time. Nicotine pouches drive the estimated growth, while TPB’s legacy products don’t seem likely to grow very meaningfully anymore.

I now expect slightly lower profitability than previously due to heavy marketing investments. I expect the EBIT margin to slowly rebound to 23%, but that profitability will continue to decline slightly in 2026 before recovering.

I have kept my cash flow conversion estimates the same. I account for 50% minority interests in the Alp brand in the cash flow conversion.

The estimates put TPB’s fair value at $107.5, very near the stock price as of writing. The valuation provides another reason for TPB’s stark post-earnings reaction – the market had already started to price in very ambitious growth expectations, making any small weakness a more significant concern. After the post-earnings crash, I believe that TPB is now fairly valued.

The fair value estimate is up from $83.9 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.73%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I now assume TPB will ultimately pay off its interest-bearing debt. For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.07% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.66. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 8.73%.

Takeaway

TPB’s growth story is now solely focused on nicotine pouches as the company is capturing market share in the rapidly growing product category. Growth remained great in Q4, and increasing distribution paves a path for further growth, but profitability pressure from increased marketing and a conservative 2026 growth guidance clearly weighed on the market’s sentiment after Q4 earnings. I believe that the stock is now priced fairly after the post-Q4 correction, as the pre-earnings valuation seemed overly ambitious; I remain at a Hold rating for TPB.

data.title: Sodexo: A 6% Yielding Compounder Trading At A 60% Discount To Peers
data.description:
Over the decades, I've found that once a stock is making headlines, the money has already been made and future returns are likely going to disappoint. It's the quiet and steady compounders that have the potential to generate generational wealth, even though it isn't going to happen overnight.

If we can pick up great businesses at low valuations, chances are we're going to do well, even if the business only grows in the single digits. My whole portfolio consists of "boring" businesses which were picked up at exciting valuations. And even though those companies don't report record-breaking growth rates, my portfolio is outperforming the major indices and has done so for quite some time.

A portfolio of above-average companies bought at below-average valuations usually comes with a high starting dividend yield. This way, we're better protected against the wild swings in investor sentiment that undeniably happen over years and decades. It's mostly the performance of the underlying businesses, and not the mood of investors, that determines our future returns.

Sodexo: A Steady Company At Rock-Bottom Valuations

In the current exuberant market environment, where no price seems too high for many stocks, finding bargains isn't easy. Sodexo (SDXOF) (SDXAY), a French company that offers catering and facility management services for big organizations, is starting to look promising.

The company provides cafeteria services and manages cleaning, security, and maintenance for companies, stadiums, schools, hospitals, and senior living facilities, among others.

What's important is that Sodexo has long-running contracts with its clients that can span 5 or 10 years, which make cash flows quite stable and predictable. The retention rate of those contracts is usually well above 90% adding to the stability.

Sodexo's share price has been declining for years, and the company is now trading near record-low valuations and, subsequently, near the highest dividend yield in its history.

The dividend yield sits at 6%, above the yield during the COVID crash, and twice as high as the average yield provided over the last two decades.

Trading at a P/E ratio of 9, the market is pessimistic about Sodexo's future earnings and cash flows.

While there are no risk-free investments, and declining profitability or dividend cuts are always a possibility, I believe those fears are overblown.

Sodexo is the second-largest player in its industry, right after Compass Group. Sodexo's margins and efficiency lag those of its larger rival, but nonetheless, the French company keeps growing, as most contracts include annual price increases tied to inflation indices.

Over the last two decades, the company has always generated positive FCF in any single year, even during the COVID crisis. The all-important FCF/share increased steadily over longer time frames. In 2024, Sodexo spun off Pluxee, therefore, the dip in FCF/share in 2025 isn't worrisome. More on the spinoff later on.

Trading at a 6% dividend yield, we'd only need a low- to mid-single digit growth rate in order to achieve double-digit total returns. That's roughly the average inflation rate, and it's the average growth rate we should expect from a company like Sodexo over the long term.

Expected Total Returns Going Forward

As long as we're paying low valuations, even a low-growth company like Sodexo can be a decent long-term investment. If we factor in a potential multiple expansion over the next decade, let's say from the current 9x earnings to a more reasonable 14x, total returns could be quite good.

6% dividend yield + 3% growth + 4.52% multiple expansion = 13.52% expected total return per year

In a world, where most markets seem overvalued and the S&P 500 is estimated to generate negative nominal total returns going forward, an expected double-digit total return from a stable company like Sodexo seems attractive.

What Led to the Selloff?

The significant re-rating of Sodexo started in March 2025. The company reported softer trends in the US, and had to lower its guidance. Shares tanked 17% that day, and since then have been in a steady downtrend.

Guidance for this year has been relatively weak, as organic revenue is expected to only grow between 1.5% and 2.5% with slightly lower operating profit margins than last fiscal year.

It's expected that the coming fiscal year will be a transition year, something impatient investors hate to hear. So, higher investments will likely weigh on the short-term outlook.

On top of that, the U.S. dollar has been weak and the U.S. is Sodexo's largest market. The company reports in Euro, therefore profits will likely come in lower this year.

As it's often the case, if a company cuts its guidance, investors sell. Lower prices lead investment banks to cut their price targets, which attracts more sellers.

I believe the selling is overdone. Short-term panic often creates long-term opportunities. After all, Sodexo is a historically stable and highly cash-generative company now trading at single-digit P/E ratios.

Pluxee Spinoff

Another factor that may be weighing on the stock is the spinoff of Sodexo's higher growth business segment in 2024 that is now called Pluxee. For every Sodexo share, investors received one Pluxee share.

After the spinoff, the new Sodexo is solely focused on catering and facility management, which is labor-intensive and naturally has lower margins and slower growth. In this exuberant market environment which favors growth stocks, this may weigh on the sentiment, but I believe the spinoff wasn't bad idea.

Since the spinoff, Pluxee shares have plummeted by more than 60% and are now trading at similar P/E ratios as Sodexo. Regulatory risks are weighing on the sector, as the market leader Edenred faced a similar decline in share price.

Pluxee faces more regulatory risks than Sodexo and is heavily exposed to single country risks. As an example, Brazil is a critical market for Pluxee and is responsible for about one-third of the company's revenues.

Not long ago, Brazil has introduced new rules that could lead to significantly lower profits for Pluxee. Italy and France are also implementing new regulations and taxes that weigh on the sector.

By spinning off Pluxee, Sodexo should be more resilient as it faces fewer regulatory risks and isn't distracted from its core business. In comparison to Pluxee, Sodexo's revenues seem well-diversified and stable.

66% of Sodexo's revenues come from catering ad the remaining 34% are generated by facility management services.

Compared to Pluxee, Sodexo is better diversified geographically. The U.S. is its largest market, followed by Europe and the rest of the world.

In comparison, Pluxee is heavily concentrated in Europe and Latin America.

So, overall, I view Sodexo as the safer and more compelling investment going forward, even if the expected growth rates will likely be lower than Pluxee's.

More growth-focused investors could still own the "old" Sodexo by buying one Pluxee share for every Sodexo share. Personally, that's the way I am investing in the company, as I buy one share of Pluxee for every share of Sodexo.

I am well aware that Pluxee will likely be more volatile and risky, but the higher expected growth rates over the long term may compensate for some of the risks involved. Since Pluxee shares currently trade around €11 and Sodexo at €45, my Sodexo position is four times larger and that's the way I like it as a risk-averse investor.

Valuation Comparison Against Peers

If we compare Sodexo's valuation against its peers, it seems like the market is pessimistic about Sodexo, but not the industry.

Compass Group, the largest player in the industry, is trading at 28x earnings. A higher growth rate, higher margins, and larger scale warrant a premium, but I believe the market is too optimistic about Compass Group and too pessimistic about Sodexo.

It reminds me of the situation of British American Tobacco (BAT) and Philip Morris a year or two ago. BAT was trading at 8x earnings and Philip Morris over 30x earnings. Since then, BAT has outperformed Philip Morris by a wide margin on a total return basis, even though the underlying business is not growing very fast.

A great company can be a bad investment at the wrong price. And a mediocre company can be a great investment at the right price.

Another listed company in the industry is Aramark. The company generates roughly two-thirds of Sodexo's revenues and showed similar growth rates over the last decade. Overall, Aramark's FCF generation over the long term was more erratic compared to Sodexo's cash flows.

One could argue that Sodexo is the more stable company historically, yet Aramark is trading at 35x earnings. Sodexo is trading at roughly one-third of the valuation metric compared to its listed peers.

In my opinion, this wide valuation gap isn't warranted, and I wouldn't be surprised if the gap narrows in the years ahead. On top of that, we get the 6% dividend yield, which is well above its peers. Aramark's dividend yield sits at 1.16% and Compass Group at 2.15%.

Debt Levels

Despite the low valuation, Sodexo's debt levels are reasonable. Long-term credit ratings range from Baa1 to BBB+.

At the end of fiscal year 2025, the company had €2.7 billion in net debt for a net debt/EBITDA ratio of 1.8x. Net debt/FCF stands around 4.4x, which is a little higher than I'd like, but it's still reasonable given the defensive business model.

Risk Factors

I believe Sodexo offers a compelling risk-reward profile at current valuations, but no investment is risk-free. There's a lot that could go wrong with this investment over the long term, and here are some risk factors that I perceive to be most important.

Sodexo operates in a fragmented and competitive industry. The company lives off its long-term relationships with its clients, and there's always the risk of losing contracts to competitors. With more than 400,000 employees, the business model relies on skilled labor and a shortage could hurt the company.

Due to the high competition and the labor intensity in the catering and facility management industry, margins tend to be low. Sodexo's net margin was just 2.88% in 2025. Thin margins can turn to steep losses, when revenues decline or costs rise, but the company is somewhat protected by long-term contracts that usually include inflation protection.

Providing services to offices and enterprises are almost 50% of Sodexo's revenues. Since COVID there has been a trend to remote working. If this trend continues, it could have a negative effect on the company.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the market seems to be punishing Sodexo for its guidance cut and a few short-term headwinds. While everyone else seems to be chasing "priced-for-perfection" growth stories, it's those stable, cash-generative businesses bought at low multiples that could offer stability once the investor sentiment turns.

Sodexo isn't a stock that's going to double overnight, but patient long-term investors can lock in a nearly 6% dividend yield at current prices. Add in some low-to-mid single digit growth and potential multiple expansion, and total returns could be in the double-digits going forward.

The stock is trading at a fraction of what peers like Compass Group and Aramark command, and offers a decent margin of a safety in a world where finding businesses trading below intrinsic value is increasingly difficult. More often than not, it's those overlooked and "boring" companies bought at attractive valuations, that generate the exciting long-term returns.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Paysafe: 2026 Outlook Confirms The Sky Is Not Falling
data.description:
Introduction

Software stocks have been under pressure in 2026, impacted by fears that AI may make many software offerings irrelevant while at the same time breaking barriers to entry, thus increasing competition in the sector and putting pressure on margins.

Against this backdrop, investors are paying close attention to management outlooks for 2026 for any signs of growth deceleration. Paysafe (PSFE) anticipates revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth of about 5-8% this year, confirming a convergence between organic and reported growth that started in recent quarters.

While Paysafe is not alone in offering attractive valuations in the software sector, my rating remains a Buy, with the positive outlook underpinned by:

  • A 10.7% free cash flow yield to enterprise value, quite attractive in light of revenue growth above the pace of inflation.
  • A feasible path to achieving lower net leverage by the end of 2026, driven by adjusted EBITDA growth and debt principal paydowns.
  • Room for an extra $50-60 million in share buybacks, potentially allowing Paysafe to retire a low double-digit percentage of shares outstanding.

Readers not familiar with Paysafe may find the Company Overview section in my previous article insightful. The only notable change I would highlight is that net debt now accounts for roughly 84% of the company's $2.8 billion enterprise value, with shares outstanding down following Q4 2025 share buybacks.

Q4 2025 Results Overview

Paysafe reported 4.4% Y/Y revenue growth in the final quarter of 2025, driven by ongoing payment volume growth (+9.8% Y/Y), particularly in the digital wallets segment. Margin performance however was underwhelming, with the adjusted EBITDA margin slipping 1.3% Y/Y to 23.3%. As such, Paysafe posted a 1.2% decline in adjusted EBITDA despite robust topline growth.

Cash flow conversion was a bright spot, with the unleveraged free cash flow surging 46% Y/Y to $103 million, bringing full-year free cash flow to $298 million. Adjusted EPS was $0.46/share in the quarter, down 4.2% Y/Y, taking full-year 2025 adjusted EPS to $1.95/share, down 8.9% Y/Y. The decline in EPS reflects the effect of lower margins, only partially offset by a 10% decline in shares outstanding to 55.7 million thanks to aggressive share buybacks.

Overall I would say that Paysafe's results were quite reasonable when we factor in that Q4 2025 was the final quarter where year-over-year comparisons were impacted by 2024 divestments. As such, as I will discuss in the next section, topline growth will increasingly reflect strong organic revenue growth, which stood at 4% in Q4 2025 and 5% in full-year 2025.

2026 Outlook and Leverage

Paysafe expects revenue and adjusted EBITDA growth of 5% to 8% in 2026, with the company conservatively factoring in flat margins. Even so, adjusted EPS is seen growing in the double-digits, with the outlook based on 57 million shares outstanding, somewhat higher than the 55.7 million in Q4 2025. I expect Paysafe to remain active in repurchasing shares, likely resulting in a somewhat lower share count by the end of 2026.

The company will also need to prioritize debt repayments, as the net leverage (calculated as the net debt of $2,365 million divided by the adjusted EBITDA of $429 million) stood at 5.5x at the end of 2025, up from 4.7x in 2024. Paysafe targets a multiple below 5x by the end of 2026.

Taking into account the adjusted EBITDA range of $449-464 million in 2026, we see that EBITDA growth alone should be enough to bring down net leverage to about 5.2x. The remainder of the decrease can be achieved through debt principal paydowns, while still allowing for roughly $50-60 million in share buybacks, enough to retire a low double-digit percentage of shares outstanding.

I would caution however that buybacks are likely to be tilted toward H2 2026, dependent on the execution of the full-year outlook.

Valuation and Rating

Paysafe trades at roughly 3.8x its trailing adjusted earnings, with the low multiple largely reflecting the company's very high leverage (net debt accounts for 84% of the company's $2.8 billion enterprise value).

Given this capital structure, I believe it is best to approach Paysafe's valuation from an enterprise-level perspective. The company achieved $298 million in unleveraged free cash flow in 2025, reflecting cash flow conversion of adjusted EBITDA of 69%, 3% higher than the 66% achieved in 2024. Taking into account Paysafe's 2026 outlook, I believe that free cash flow of around $300 million is certainly achievable this year as well.

Relative to the $2.8 billion enterprise value, we see that the free cash flow yield stands at about 10.7%, which for a company growing above the rate of inflation is quite good.

The free cash flow yield to equity is naturally much higher, at about 39%, as Paysafe only anticipates roughly $133 million in interest expenses this year. This should leave approximately $167 million in discretionary cash flows, available for debt principal paydowns and the aforementioned share buybacks.

All in all, as long as Paysafe keeps up with robust operating momentum, the stock clearly remains in the value category camp, thus earning a Buy rating from me. I would however note that Paysafe's attractive valuation is now characteristic of the whole software sector. Case in point, one of its largest competitors, PayPal (PYPL), currently trades at a free cash flow yield of about 15%, with the company achieving a comparable 4% Y/Y revenue growth in Q4 2025.

As such, I also see the rationale of making the switch from Paysafe to PayPal stock, although the latter arguably deserves a lower valuation due to weaker adjusted EPS growth expectations in 2026. In essence, Paysafe is now the steady growth company, while PayPal has turned into somewhat of a turnaround bet.

Risks

With net debt accounting for 84% of Paysafe's enterprise value, a favorable financing environment remains paramount in the investment case. As such, slower Fed rate cuts and/or renewed ECB rate hikes to combat energy-driven inflation may increase interest expenses. The silver lining is that such an increase may prove short-lived as energy prices will likely stabilize over the medium term.

As we have seen with PayPal, a notable slowdown in operating momentum may have highly negative consequences for Paysafe's valuation. As such, delivering on the 2026 outlook is essential, especially if Paysafe wants to continue with share buybacks. Indeed I expect the company to prioritize debt principal repayments in light of its lower year-end leverage target, likely indicating that any underperformance relative to expectations will affect share repurchases, as discussed on the conference call:

While in 2025, we allocated more than $90 million to share repurchases, we are laser-focused on reducing our net leverage ratio in 2026 and expect to be below 5x by the end of this year. We repaid $64 million of our revolver in the month of January. And while we continue to think our shares are materially undervalued, we will prioritize debt repayment this year.

Conclusion

Amid recent market volatility, Paysafe's 20% surge on Q4 2025 earnings seems excessive. I would however note that it is largely the result of the company's debt-heavy capital structure, making significant share price swings needed to bring a sizable move in the overall enterprise-level valuation.

Paysafe continues to grow at a pace above the level of inflation, with the 2026 outlook soothing any concerns investors may have had amid broad weakness in the software sector. I estimate that higher adjusted EBITDA and debt principal paydowns should allow Paysafe to reach its lower leverage targets for 2026.

Assuming Paysafe delivers on its outlook, I also see room for an extra $50-60 million in share buybacks, likely enough to retire a low double-digit percentage of shares outstanding. Investors will likely have to stomach H1 2026 volatility as I see potential buybacks tilted toward H2 2026.

Key risks to the investment case are Paysafe's high leverage as well as the company's ability to continue to deliver robust revenue growth amid significant competition in the payments sector.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: EPR Properties: While The Market Does Nothing, This High Yielder Is Outperforming
data.description:
The market has been pretty flat this year. Up, down, up, down, with no real direction. But that doesn't mean your portfolio has to follow that pattern. EPR Properties (EPR) stock has a total return of over 21% so far this year, while the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) has returned 0%. During this volatility, it has proven itself as a market rotation play into value and dividends.

EPR Properties is an experiential REIT. It leases out properties (triple-net leases) that offer experiences, such as movie theaters, ski resorts, gyms, and more. And its dividend yield is a relatively high 6.2%, with the most recent dividend increase of 5.1% happening last week.

Some investors might not like EPR because of its high exposure to movie theaters, particularly AMC Entertainment (AMC). However, the company is reducing its theater exposure, and I'd argue that EPR can still collect its rent payments in the meantime. Also, EPR Properties expects to invest $400-500 million this year, which can support growth.

Overall, for income investors interested in high-yield REITs, EPR Properties provides a good mix of high yield and growth potential for the industry, as well as good stock price and business momentum. The dividend also looks safe at the moment, with a Q4 AFFO payout ratio of around 68%.

Therefore, I believe it deserves a Buy rating.

Breaking Down EPR Properties' Business

What does this experiential REIT invest in, and how much exposure does it have to each industry? Let's find out.

As a percentage of annualized adjusted EBITDAre, theaters make up 36% of EPR's portfolio, Eat & Play makes up 25%, and Attractions & Cultural real estate makes up 13%. The remainder can be seen below. 94% of the annualized adjusted EBITDAre comes from experiential real estate, while the other 6% comes from its education portfolio.

Its top tenants as a percentage of total revenue can be seen below, with Topgolf accounting for 14.6% of Q4 revenue, while AMC and Regal Entertainment Group (another theater chain) made up 13.7% and 10.2% of Q4 revenue, respectively.

What To Make Of The Theater Exposure

If you're new to this company, you might be thinking, "36% exposure to theaters? No thanks." That's fine, but let's be fair.

The company clearly stated that it intends to reduce its exposure to this industry (along with a reduction in the education portfolio) while growing its other experiential investments.

In the meantime, theaters just need to make enough money to pay rent. They don't need to be the next Nvidia (NVDA) to make their payments. I've also seen people talk about how AI content can disrupt theaters, but I believe that's overstated, as a big chunk of going to the theater is to "go out" and have an experience. If that wasn't the case, Netflix (NFLX) would have killed all theaters by now.

But the sore thumb that sticks out is AMC Entertainment. The company is bleeding cash, with a negative $365.9 million in free cash flow for 2025.

Meanwhile, AMC has a cash position of just $428.5 million and long-term debt of over $4 billion, with an interest coverage ratio of 0.1x. At the current cash-burn rate, it has just enough money to survive a bit over a year. So, what do we make of that info?

Well, there are a few things to note. First, based on the consensus of two analysts, AMC's forecasted FCF for the year is -$102.2 million. That would be a major improvement if AMC can even get somewhere close to that number, and it'd give the company more than just a year of cash runway. I'm not entirely optimistic that FCF will improve that greatly, but it at least is nice to see estimates trending in the right direction.

Also, last month, AMC filed "an omnibus shelf registration covering common and preferred stock, subscription rights, depositary shares, warrants and units, and also registered an at-the-market follow-on common equity offering of up to US$150 million."

In other words, it can raise up to $150 million, which would give it that extra bit of liquidity to last a little bit longer while EPR reduces its theater exposure.

So, an AMC bankruptcy isn't imminent, as it may take over a year or two or longer to happen, if it does. But even if it does go bankrupt, that doesn't mean EPR is doomed, which brings me to my next section.

Tenant Bankruptcy Isn't The End Of The World

Regal Entertainment Group filed for bankruptcy in September 2022, and EPR ended up just fine. To give you some extra context, according to the 2022 annual report, "For the year ended December 31, 2022, approximately $94.5 million, or 14.4% and $90.7 million or 13.8% of the Company's total revenue was from AMC and Regal, respectively."

Regal accounted for 13.8% of 2022 revenue, declared bankruptcy, and still didn't cause EPR's AFFO to tank. EPR actually ended up announcing a restructuring agreement with Regal in June of 2023, where it took back 16 of the 57 theaters.

Take a look at EPR's AFFO per share history since Fiscal 2021. 2023's AFFO ended up improving compared to the prior year, and it has been relatively stable since.

At the end of the day, EPR owns the properties. A bankruptcy of one of their tenants doesn't mean a permanent loss of capital, as there are actions EPR can take, such as taking back theaters, selling them, or finding new tenants.

For example, during the Nareit REITweek: 2025 Investor Conference from June 2025, Executive VP & Chief Investment Officer Gregory E. Zimmerman stated:

One, as a result of the Regal bankruptcy and the restructuring we did with AMC, we took back a number of theaters with the thought that we would sell them over time. And over the past 4 years, we've sold 27 theaters. So right now, as it stands, we only have 3 vacant theaters. And of those, we have contracts of sale on 2 of them. So we're quite pleased with the results on that.

Not All Of Its Theater Exposure Is Bad

High theater exposure can be scary, but not all of it is bad. Cinemark (CNK) made up 5.9% of Q4 revenues, and I don't mind that because Cinemark actually makes money. In the past 10 years, it had positive FCF every year except during COVID in 2020, which makes sense.

When EPR announced the restructuring deal with Regal, it let Cinemark operate four of the 16 theaters it took back from Regal. That's something that can potentially happen again if AMC goes bankrupt.

The Positives

Now that we've talked about the elephant in the room, we can discuss more of the positives, which are its high yield, dividend growth potential, strong momentum, and modest valuation.

1. A High-Yielding Dividend That Can Grow

EPR's dividend was recently raised from $0.30/share to $0.31/share (a 5.1% increase), and it's paid monthly. Based on the most recent price of $59.94, the forward yield is 6.2%. That's a pretty good starting point, as it's above the sector median yield of 4.57%, and it helps the stock get an A- dividend yield grade from Seeking Alpha.

I won't be surprised if more low-to-mid-single-digit increases will happen over the years since EPR has the ability to do so. Based on the midpoint of 2026's FFO guidance ($5.38, which suggests 5.1% year-over-year growth compared to $5.12 in 2025) and its new $0.31/share dividend, the forward payout ratio is around 69%, which isn't far off from current levels and is manageable due to the predictable nature of rental revenues.

More precisely, management expects FFO as adjusted per share to come in at $5.28-5.48. This signals another year of growth, as FFO as adjusted per share also grew by 5% in 2025.

The investment spending of $400-500 million should help support the growth expectations, and the disposition guidance of $25-75 million should allow them remove underperforming assets and further diversify.

2. Strong Technical And FFO Revision Momentum

I already mentioned how EPR is outperforming the market so far year-to-date, with its 21%+ total return. But it's also outperforming the iShares Core US REIT ETF (USRT), which has gained 11.6% this year as of March 2.

EPR clearly has strong technical momentum, and its FFO revision trend also shows positive momentum. I'll give you an example. Around this time last year, 2026 FFO/share was expected to be $5.16. Now, the consensus is $5.33. 2027 and 2028 estimates have followed a similar trend.

3. A Low Valuation

If analysts' estimates end up being correct, with 3-4% growth expected annually for the next three years, then EPR stock trades at around a forward price/FFO multiple of 11.15x. The 2027 price/FFO multiple then drops to 10.8x.

That's a nice forward FFO yield of around 9%, and it assumes EPR doesn't surprise analysts again. Its price/AFFO and price/FFO metrics are also noticeably lower than those of the sector median (the numbers on the far right side of the image below).

A Strong Buy Quant Rating Helps The Bull Case

The factors I mentioned above actually lead EPR stock to have a Strong Buy rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system. It gets an "A-" for valuation and growth, an "A" for profitability, a "B+" for momentum, and an "A" for revisions.

Below, you'll see that over the past three years, the Quant system almost always gave it a Buy or Strong Buy rating.

And that rating has been reliable for EPR stock, since its three-year total return is 80%.

Meanwhile, the iShares Core US REIT ETF returned 31.5% over the same period.

The Risks

Of course, the first risk is the theater exposure, which I already discussed. Next, you have the overall cyclicality of experiential businesses. These businesses tend not to perform that well during recessions, so if your outlook on the economy is sour, I wouldn't recommend this stock. Past crises have sent this stock crashing fast.

Also, the company isn't absolutely rock-solid in terms of dividend safety, especially due to the nature of its business, but it's not horrible either. I think the dividend safety grade of C+ captures the risk accurately. Notably, its forward AFFO payout ratio of 68.77% is below the sector median, but what really brings the score down is the elevated net long-term debt to assets ratio of 49.8% compared to the sector median of 39.1%.

Past economic shocks have come with dividend cuts, so that's something to keep in mind as well, although the company always ends up recovering eventually.

The Bottom Line On EPR Stock

EPR Properties looks like a worthwhile pick for income investors, with its 6.2%-yielding dividend that has the potential to keep growing in the low-to-mid single digits, which is a good growth rate when combined with the high yield. The stock also has strong technical and FFO revision momentum, as well as a low forward price/FFO valuation, giving investors a 9% forward FFO yield.

It's not the safest REIT out there because its tenants operate in industries that aren't well-protected against economic shocks, but it has been an outperformer in the recent past, and I believe it can continue being one. If it doesn't outperform the S&P 500 from here, I at least expect it to outperform the REIT sector, assuming an economic crash doesn't take place. In the current environment, EPR gets a Buy rating from me.

data.title: EV Wars In February: BYD Sales Slump, NIO Sales Soar
data.description:
Article Thesis

There is a lot of competition in the global market for electric vehicles, as start-ups, legacy players, and so on roll out more and more new vehicles while expanding their production capacity. The fights for market share have gotten quite intense, and due to added pressures from the Chinese New Year effect and less political support, some EV players experienced major sales declines in February. In this report, I will take a look at how some of the most important EV companies fared in February.

Past Coverage

I have written about the EV industry here on Seeking Alpha in the past, e.g. a little more than a month ago, when I published this macro article. I also did in-depth articles on some EV players in the past, such as Tesla (TSLA), which I covered earlier this year as well.

Market Overview

While electric vehicles are sold in many markets around the world, not all of them are equally important -- quite the contrary is true, as the Chinese EV market is the most important one by far. If the Chinese EV market takes a downturn, that's a major growth headwind for almost all EV players, especially the Chinese ones, which oftentimes sell a pretty large portion of their vehicles in their home market. In China, 2026 saw the expiration of some EV subsidies, such as them no longer being sales-tax-free, unlike in the past. This resulted in some demand pull-forward in 2025, similar to what we saw in the US in Q3 2025, but it also means that 2026 hasn't been great for the Chinese EV industry so far. In China, the Chinese New Year holidays, which this year started on February 17, also tend to be a factor, as it can result in both reduced production levels as well as in reduced sales volumes.

How Did The Major EV Players Fare In February?

Let's now take a look at some of the most important EV players -- at least those that report monthly sales data. Tesla, for example, only offers quarterly sales numbers, so we don't know how well it fared in February in total, although there is some data on some individual countries.

BYD

BYD (BYDDY)(BYDDF) is a leading EV player in both China and the world, but February was a pretty disappointing month for the company. BYD sold a total of 188,000 vehicles in February, which was down by a whopping 41% compared to one year earlier. In 2025, the Chinese New Year holidays started earlier, in January, but the majority of the holiday impact was still in February last year, too, so the Chinese New Year impact alone does not explain why BYD's sales declined so much. Production was down by almost 50%, dropping even more than sales volumes -- this at least means that BYD's inventory declined, which is good for its cash flows. To me, it looks like BYD is feeling a major impact from less political tailwinds for the EV industry -- due to its value approach, higher sales taxes seem to have a pretty sizeable impact on BYD, as its customers oftentimes are price-sensitive.

NIO

NIO (NIO) is from China, too, but it operates with a very different focus compared to BYD. Instead of offering value vehicles, it offers higher-end, premium vehicles at way higher price points. Its customers are, on average, less price-sensitive, so they could better stomach the tax increase -- which helps explain why NIO did a lot better than BYD growth-wise. Its sales volumes in February were almost 60% higher than one year earlier, with sales climbing to 21,000. This was still down compared to January, but the year-over-year trajectory remained very positive, which is important. The majority of its sales came from its NIO brand, while ONVO and Firefly contributed a little less than 3,000 vehicles to the company's overall sales last month. While NIO has had its problems in the past, I like the recent trajectory -- the company seemingly has managed to come up with an attractive product portfolio that is well-received by affluent Chinese consumers.

Li Auto

Li Auto (LI) is another Chinese EV player that has seen its performance come in between BYD and NIO -- it was neither especially strong nor especially bad, with sales rising by 1% versus last year's February, to 26,400. The 1% year-over-year sales increase is a better result compared to January, when Li Auto had to report a small year-over-year decline -- momentum thus looks positive, although this, of course, doesn't guarantee that sales volumes will continue to improve going forward. During the second quarter, Li Auto plans to release the new Li L9 SUV, which may help keep the recent positive momentum in place, but competitors are naturally rolling out new and updated models in the coming months as well.

XPeng

XPeng (XPEV) is from China, too, and it did, like BYD, have a pretty tough month -- its sales declined to just above 15,000 vehicles, down by a massive 49.9%. This was an even larger relative decline compared to BYD, and unlike BYD, XPeng isn't profitable, so the risk of financial difficulties is more pronounced. Management sees a major Chinese New Year impact, but as the results of NIO and Li prove, that alone does not explain why XPEV's sales declined so much. XPeng seeks to boost its exports through 2026, which may help improve its performance in the coming months, but at least right now, XPeng doesn't look strong at all.

Xiaomi

Xiaomi (XIACY) is not an EV pure-play, but has still become a major player in a very short period of time. In February, it sold more than 20,000 vehicles, down around 15% versus 2025 -- as Xiaomi has delivered very strong growth before, this is a bit of a let-down, but Xiaomi still managed to fare a lot better than BYD, despite also being focused on the value market. It will be interesting to see whether Xiaomi is able to resume growth in March, to get back on a high-growth track, or whether its growth has stalled out for now.

Geely

Geely (GELYF) sells both EVs and ICE-powered vehicles across a range of brands, including Geely, LYNK, and ZEEKR. In February, pure EV sales were down by a couple of percentage points, but its plug-in sales soared by almost 90%, rising to 50,000 for the month. Growth was strong for the ZEEKR brand, where sales jumped by 70% -- a very strong result, considering the negative macro environment with the new sales tax and the Chinese New Year festivities. The main brand, Geely, experienced a sales decline, which had also been the case in January -- ZEEKR, a premium brand, faring better, could be explained by ZEEKR buyers being less price-sensitive and not minding the sales tax too much.

Who Is Winning The EV War?

Growth-wise, February was a strong month for both NIO and Geely's ZEEKR, which had also been the case in January. Mass-market, value manufacturers such as BYD and Geely's main brand fared worse, at least partially due to less political support for EVs in China. NIO, despite its strong growth, has profitability issues, however.

BYD and Xiaomi have a lot of resources, which should be an advantage for them, even though both experienced a sales decline in February. All in all, there is no clear winner in the EV wars right now -- competition is intense, margins are weak, and it's hard to forecast which company will ultimately win out, as there also isn't a lot of brand loyalty.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Income Recipe: Balancing Stability And Spice For A 10% Yield
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

One of my hobbies is cooking. After a hard day of work, there is something that I find relaxing about completely shifting my focus from numbers to the art of cooking. For some, cooking is just a necessity to sustain oneself. I know people with the idea that "cooking" is following the directions on a package and putting it on a plate.

For me, cooking is an art. Finding the right balance of herbs and spices to bring out the right notes of sweet, sour, bitter, salty, and umami to work together in harmony to create a meal that is worth remembering. Let's be honest, I'm not going to be competing on Iron Chef. I've had more than one dish that I put a lot of time into, only for it to turn out to be inedible. But you don't need to be Michelangelo to pursue painting as a hobby.

When cooking, it isn't about one ingredient. While you might have one ingredient that takes center stage, a delicious dish comes from the supporting ingredients that create the stage and highlight the best flavors from the star.

Building an investment portfolio isn't all that different. Investors often focus on the picks; they want to know what "the best" ticker to buy is, and they often judge an investment based on its individual performance. Yet, a portfolio is not a single ticker any more than a world-class meal is a single ingredient. A well-constructed portfolio is one that has balance. Where the risks of one investment overlap with the strengths of another investment. If we hold investments that are interest rate sensitive, we also want investments that aren't. We don't want a portfolio reliant upon one star stock, because then we're in trouble if that one investment turns out to be rotten. We want a portfolio of investments that complement and support each other, working together in harmony to achieve our goal.

Here is our recipe for investment success.

The Goal

First, what are we trying to make? There is more than one way to make money in the stock market. So we should determine what specifically we want from our portfolio.

Our goal is to generate a current income stream that averages an 8%-10% yield and grows every year.

Our logic behind this goal is straightforward. When you retire, what are you losing? You are losing an income stream. That paycheck you were getting regularly will be gone, and at best, it is being replaced by a smaller and probably less frequent check. Yet, even though your monthly income goes down, the monthly expenses remain on the same schedule. So, our goal is to close that gap and be able to withdraw as much or more income than the missing paycheck.

This means that we need our portfolio to produce substantial cash flow on a regular and recurring basis. Additionally, we need to account for inflation, so we need that income to grow, and we know the world isn't perfect – so we need a safety cushion.

The Cuisine

To achieve the goals outlined above, we use what we call the Income Method – a basic investment outline that provides the guidelines and structure we will follow to achieve our goal.

  1. Buy investments paying a high yield today that are well-supported by the fundamentals.
  2. Focus on growing the income your portfolio produces.
  3. Diversify your portfolio with at least 42 different holdings.
  4. Reinvest a portion of your income. We recommend at least 25%.
  5. Never spend more than your income.

Note that this investment strategy doesn't pigeonhole you into one type of investment or into specific picks. This is the big picture: general guidelines that will guide our decisions when looking at the wealth of ingredients available in the market.

We're going to the market, and we have $500,000. Now, let's look at the ingredients we're going to buy.

The Starch

$150,000 in fixed income. Average yield ~8% – $12,000/year in cash flow.

Our goal is to generate a recurring income stream, so it makes a lot of sense to have a base that generates a predictable, stable, and recurring stream of interest and dividends.

For our portfolio, this means preferred stocks and baby bonds. Yes, I know these stocks are probably going to underperform the S&P 500 or whatever equity index you want to compare it to. The reason is simple: these aren't common equities, and their purpose isn't to achieve unlimited price upside. These investments have a par value, and with a few exceptions, their price upside will be limited once the price is over par. These are investments that are lower risk than common equities, and in general, lower risk means a lower reward.

The purpose of having a portion of our portfolio in fixed income is not because we believe fixed income is going to outperform common equities. Over the long run, they probably won't.

What fixed income does for our portfolio is far more important – it helps ensure that cash flow in our portfolio remains robust. Our fixed-income portfolio has a current average yield of 8%.

One example is Pebblebrook Hotel Trust 6.375% Series G, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (PEB.PR.G), which is a preferred equity for an internally managed hotel REIT with 44 properties located across major U.S. gateway cities and resort destinations, including California, Florida, Boston, Washington, D.C., San Diego, and other coastal markets. The portfolio consists primarily of upscale and luxury hotels, with a 50/50 mix of leisure and business customers. COVID-19 was a black swan that materially impacted hotels. PEB was impacted and slashed the common dividend to $0.01/quarter. Yet, it continued to pay the preferred shares that it had outstanding at that time without interruption.

PEB-G is trading below par, yields ~8%, and provides some room for capital gains if interest rates decline. Yet, its real value is having a dividend that we can rely on even when the world goes sideways.

The Sides

$150k in funds, including ETFs and CEFs, average yield ~9% – $13,500/year in cash

Funds can be a great way to provide diversity to a portfolio. They can provide exposure to sectors that don't otherwise pay dividends, and they come in a wide variety of flavors. We have holdings that invest in debt, REITs, large caps, utilities, infrastructure, and more.

abrdn World Healthcare Fund (THW), currently yielding about 11%, is a good example of a fund that increases our diversity with global exposure. Approximately half of THW's exposure is outside the U.S., and a large portion of its U.S. holdings also have international revenues. Our portfolio does tend to have a strong US bias, so a holding like THW can provide us with the variety we don't have in our individual picks.

THW’s top three sectors are pharmaceuticals, biotech, and healthcare equipment. Source

This adds diversity to our portfolio while also providing the cash flow that achieves our goals.

The Protein

$150k in individual picks, average yield ~9% – $13,500/year in cash

The "stars" of the dish are the individual picks. These are companies that are paying a high yield and we believe have a bright future. They have a higher risk than a fund that holds hundreds of stocks or fixed-income investments that have a stable and more predictable cash flow. Companies can change the dividend in their common equity whenever they want, for whatever reason they want. Sometimes they are forced to by circumstances; sometimes they just change their strategy.

We like to have a combination of investments that provide a high yield right now and some holdings that raise their dividends frequently to provide an element of dividend growth to our portfolio. A "surf & turf," if you will.

For example, you can pair Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY), a mortgage REIT that invests in agency MBS on a leveraged basis, with Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), an MLP that uses a conservative business model, fully self-funding maintenance and growth CapEx from cash flow.

NLY pays a high dividend that is relatively variable because they are passing along the results of its MBS trade, which vary with conditions. NLY currently yields 12%.

EPD starts with a lower yield but has raised its dividend every year since 1998. It currently yields 6%. Combined, we achieve a yield of 9%, above our target.

By combining the two, we get a high level of current income plus growth while combining two companies that have completely unrelated businesses.

The Spice

$50k in nosebleed yields, average yield ~20% – $10,000/year in cash.

Sometimes, the market gives you investment opportunities that will be very volatile but have very high yields. These holdings span various investment classes, but all share the feature of being very high-yielding. Sometimes these are companies that are experiencing a period of high uncertainty.

For example, BRC Group Holdings (RILY) bonds like RILYT are still trading at a 26% yield to maturity. They were a lot higher when many feared that bankruptcy was a real threat. They aren't completely out of the woods, but the picture has improved dramatically, and the bond prices have rallied to match.

These investments can pay off handsomely to investors who can stand the heat but do fall into the category of "being high yield for a reason." CLO equity is a sector that always has a nosebleed yield and has recently seen massive swings in valuations. The decision by these funds to retain more capital by reducing dividends has caused them to trade at their lowest valuations in their history, including during COVID.

The potential reward for these investments when things calm down is huge. Yet holding them isn't always easy. These holdings might make up a small portion of the portfolio, but they hog 90% of the attention in chat – just like that hot sauce.

Make sure you are choosing holdings that match your tolerance.

Customize Your Own Dish

The recipe above generates a cash flow of $49,000/year for a 9.8% yield. But as with cooking, feel free to customize it to your needs and your preferences. Some people can't handle spice at all. They prefer to avoid the jalapeño peppers. Ok, then take the 10% allocated to spice and invest it in more starch.

Then your portfolio would be:

$200k in fixed income at 8%. ($16,000)

$150k in funds at 9%. ($13,500)

$150k in individual picks at 9%. ($13,500)

This adds up to $43,000, or an average yield of 8.6%. A lower yield, with lower risk and less stress. But still, well within the goal.

The point is that you don't need to blindly follow a recipe on a box. When building your portfolio, have a structure to follow and customize your portfolio to your needs. The important thing to understand is what role each holding is playing in your portfolio.

Is it serving as the base to provide a reliable income?

Is it providing you with a high amount of cash flow right now?

Is it providing an element of dividend growth to your portfolio to help your income grow in the future?

Is it a "spicy" investment that provides massive cash flow with greater uncertainty?

A good cook adds ingredients to a dish because they serve a purpose. They aren't just randomly grabbing something and throwing it in to see what happens.

When you are constructing your portfolio, know what you are trying to create and add investments that fit to make your portfolio more likely to achieve your goals.

data.title: 3 Huge Market Risks That Keep Me Up At Night
data.description:
Introduction

First of all, I want to thank my readers for helping me break the 50,000-follower level. When I started writing for Seeking Alpha roughly a decade ago, I never thought I would make it past 1,000. Now, we’re at 50x that. And, according to the numbers, we have had average growth of at least 1,000 new followers per month for almost two straight years.

From the bottom of my heart, thank you for letting me be of service!

Soon, I’ll have some exciting announcements for you.

But first, there’s a lot more to discuss in light of these volatile markets.

I have written mostly bullish articles in recent months, for the obvious reason that I’m bullish. Despite risks like the growth recovery being on thin ice, I have made the case that stagflation and recession fears are overblown.

My thesis has been that we may be getting the “flation” part of stagflation, yet not the “stag.” So far, that thesis is looking good, as leading growth indicators have rebounded, economic growth is broadening, and not even recent weakness in consumer spending is keeping the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta’s GDPNow indicator from falling below 3.0%, as we can see below.

Moreover, as most of my regular readers may know, I am overweight in energy and industrial stocks, with all of my top five holdings (more than 50% of my portfolio) operating in a somewhat cyclical environment.

Especially because of my positioning and coverage, it is important not to lose sight of the risks under the surface. Everything else would be reckless and could potentially lead to mistakes. And, to be honest, I may be wrong. Not playing Devil’s Advocate is never a good idea.

With that said, I have brought up risks in the past, including software and Big Tech risks. These risks are a big reason why I own so many “hard asset” investments. However, that’s just the tip of the iceberg, as there are many risks under the market’s surface that, I believe, investors need to incorporate into their research to avoid potential pitfalls.

That’s what this article is about.

So, let’s get right to it, as we have a lot to discuss!

Have You Ever Heard Of The Singularity Trade?

Another part of my thesis that has come to fruition is that this is a stock-picker’s market.

This thesis was based on a number of factors, including the market’s top-heavy structure, the fact that AI isn’t a bullish-only factor anymore, as it’s now picking winners (adopters) and losers (think of software), and because we are seeing a cyclical growth upswing for the first time since 2021.

As we can see below, the correlation between S&P 500 stocks has broken down. Although I am painting with a super broad brush here, it’s roughly a quarter of what it used to be since 2010. Usually, the implied correlation index hovered close to 40. Now, it’s slightly above 10.

To me, this is just part of the thesis. As I have said before, including in podcasts, this is a fast-moving market where capital is repricing opportunities within days. That’s a way of saying whenever a market sees a risk or opportunity, it is super quick to act.

For example, Micron (MU) is now a company with a market cap of nearly $500 billion. Last year, around this time, its market cap was 25% of what it is now. This is due to the bottlenecks in memory, a major component of data center hardware.

Texas Pacific Land (TPL) is similar. As soon as the market found out its data center thesis was unfolding (bringing data centers to West Texas), its stock rose from less than $300 to more than $500 within less than two months.

Then there’s Duolingo (DUOL), the fast-growing app that helps you learn new languages. It was at $525 in 2025. Then, the market found out that there may not be a big moat in a world where software coding becomes very cheap. Now, it’s close to $100.

There are just three examples. But, in general, I think we all have examples of just how fast capital is moving. Especially in this environment, it seems like an almost binary trade, where the market either puts you in a “winner” or “loser” basket, all fueled by AI disruption.

Block Inc. (XYZ) is another excellent example, as the company announced it would cut its workforce by 40%. The market immediately rewarded that move, as the stock is now roughly 30% off its lows as of February 27. We also see that the stock has been struggling for years.

And, as you may have guessed, the move was triggered by AI:

Dorsey did not hide behind consultant-speak. In his note to employees and his shareholder letter, he made the logic explicit: “Intelligence tools have changed what it means to build and run a company. We’re already seeing it internally. A significantly smaller team, using the tools we’re building, can do more and do it better. And intelligence tool capabilities are compounding faster every week.” - Forbes

As some of my friends in the tech and investment space told me, they believe this creates a new incentive. Managers now see that the market is quickly rewarding moves that show a company is capable of shedding employees due to innovation. While it obviously needs to be seen just how successful Block will be, the initial reaction from the market was clear.

Some people will call me a drama queen, but last year, Anthropic already said that AI could wipe out close to half of all entry-level white-collar jobs and cause unemployment to rise to 10-20% over the next five years. Personally, I doubt that, as I don’t think AI adoption will be that fast, but the fact is that a lot of jobs are extremely vulnerable.

And that’s not even the main problem.

The main problem is that AI lowers the cost of everything. While it’s still somewhat early, it massively lowers the price of code, which has become a huge problem for software. While nobody can just “vibe code” a new Salesforce (CRM), Adobe (ADBE), or Intuit (INTU), all of which were among the most successful software companies before the crash, my point is that software will get cheaper, and competition will rise.

China is making this problem way worse.

For example, while China may not have the tech leadership that the U.S. has, it is now flooding the world with cheaper AI alternatives, according to Asia Times. They have super cheap open-source AI models, including programs like Seedance that can create high-quality videos. In the Asia Times article, it even said that these models are creating thousands of accounts to harvest answers from AI models like Claude.

I am bringing this up because millions of bots can build low-cost bespoke software models for whatever purpose they need. That’s the entire point of this innovation. Sure, a giant corporation will stick to a major player like Salesforce, but the masses will drown in customized AI solutions. Just last week, a developer told me he could basically copy a successful website within days. The biggest bottleneck is data access.

This is such a big risk, as data, code, and even tailored software are becoming dirt cheap.

I have said it before, and I’m saying it again, which is that I believe we are in the early innings of mass disruption on the stock market. I truly believe that the majority of S&P 500 companies will face disruption in some way. Many will survive, but almost all may have to make business model changes.

It makes stock picking fun, but it also comes with tremendous risks for the economy and the stock market.

Another Problem for Big Tech

I am not against Big Tech, even though I admit that I may be giving off these vibes.

My main reason for not owning any Big Tech isn’t just my preference for cyclical value stocks in this market, but also because AI may create a new problem for Big Tech. One of my favorite examples is Amazon (AMZN). I like this company a lot, and I actually want to buy it in the quarters/years ahead.

As I have written before, this company is one of my favorites, as it has such a massive installed base (think of its warehouses and Prime) that allows it to quickly use AI to boost its own margins. As Amazon has the lowest margins among Big Tech companies (see below) due to its consumer operations (that makes total sense), I believe efficiency gains can lead to earnings growth that most peers cannot match. That’s my thesis in a nutshell, anyway.

Source: FINVIZ

Big Tech, with inclusion from some non-tech giants, accounts for roughly 40% of the market.

That’s not a problem. However, it becomes a problem if that group suddenly loses momentum and trust from investors. Right now, that is happening, as the Magnificent Seven (that’s a good proxy for Big Tech) has seen underperformance, as the Mag-7-to-S&P 500 ratio has erased more than a year of outperformance.

The problem here isn’t software disruption but CapEx risks.

By now, most of you will know that just five hyperscalers are expected to spend nearly $700 billion on CapEx this year. As we can see below, that’s roughly 7x total 2020 CapEx.

Even Alphabet (GOOGL) is now issuing AI-related debt in the form of a 100-year bond:

Now, let’s just underline that for a second: Google, a nearly $4 trillion public company with more than $73 billion in free cash flow annually, is turning to debt markets to raise even more money. That’s because even Google’s $126 billion cash on hand starts to look pretty paltry when the company says it plans to double its AI spending this year – to a staggering $185 billion. - CNN

This isn’t bad at all. Next to Amazon, Google/Alphabet is another Big Tech stock I want to own. However, the risk here is that investors want to see a payoff on these investments.

Not only are these companies spending tons of money, but it’s also unclear how big the payoff will be. Right now, Google may be leading the AI revolution with its models, but as we can see below, competition is fierce. Who knows what this market will look like a few years from now? According to Polymarket, Anthropic may be beating Google this month.

The risk here is AI adoption. As much as I believe that AI disruption will be a major factor, slow AI adoption could hurt Big Tech returns. This is what Seeking Alpha reported in October:

However, while AI usage has surged dramatically, researchers are significantly scaling back their expectations of what AI can currently do as they gain firsthand experience, moving beyond hype toward nuanced, evidence-based adoption. Last year, researchers believed AI already outperformed humans for over half of potential use cases presented. This year, that figure dropped to less than one-third. - Seeking Alpha

We’re essentially talking about a “CapEx hangover,” which is a term you may hear a lot more in the future, as it’s basically the fear that it could lower buybacks and risk capital efficiencies of Big Tech companies.

It basically brings me back to the core of the issue:

  • Big Tech is now CapEx-heavy.
  • Competition is heating up.
  • ROIC results are uncertain.

I think stocks like AMZN and GOOGL are buyable on weakness, yet my main point here is that the engine that has carried the market is weakening.

I was Wrong About Credit

I like to mention my wins. However, I also make mistakes. Becoming bullish on private credit too early was one of them. While I like to make the case that I was early, I have to file this thesis as being wrong.

One part I got right, which is that software growth fears could hit Business Development Companies. In September, I wrote that because private credit giants had focused so much on fast-growing software, they were now prone to disruption risks themselves.

I should have stuck with that, as now even former Goldman Sachs CEO Blankfein came out to warn of these massive risks:

Concerns about the “liquidity mismatch” have been heightened by the current stresses in the US private credit market. Investors have become nervous of many US private credit funds, particularly because of their often high exposure to software companies. As software shares have tumbled on worries over the impact of AI, the shares of many quoted private credit funds have also fallen.

It also triggered redemption requests at Blue Owl Capital Corp II, an unlisted closed-end fund that offered quarterly redemptions, making it similar in some ways to an LTAF. In an attempt to stem outflows, Blue Owl, the $300bn alternative asset manager that runs Capital Corp II, proposed merging it with a quoted counterpart fund. But it was forced to back down. Instead it has permanently restricted redemptions from Capital Corp II and announced plans for a gradual liquidation of the fund. - Financial News London

This is what the BDC ETF (BIZD) looks like:

This is the chart of the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), which owns a lot of low-rated debt (in the low-B range):

Essentially, the market is fearing a chain reaction, as AI disruption has hit technology, and investors are simply unsure how big the effect on other loans could be. That’s one of the biggest risks in private credit, as we are dealing with millions of loans across countless companies. Moreover, as these deals are private, it is nearly impossible to assess the hidden risks.

Personally, I do not expect a chain reaction. However, I am also much more selective here. My game plan here is to become contrarian. In the months ahead, when my liquidity improves again, I’ll look for entries in some of the best-managed private credit companies, including Ares Management (ARES), which I consider the gold standard of private credit.

The main reason I will be looking for a buying opportunity is my bullish view on the economy. If we get further growth broadening, credit demand will remain strong, especially if it comes with higher inflation. If the market keeps de-risking these stocks, I happily buy them on weakness.

Takeaway

I am bullish on the economy and the stock market.

However, if I didn’t highlight substantial risks, I wouldn’t be doing my job.

Right now, I believe we are in a binary, AI-fueled environment where capital is moving faster than ever. Let’s call it “efficient price discovery.” To me, there are three major risks that I think investors need to understand before deploying capital:

  • Mass disruption risks of AI.
  • CapEx hangover risks.
  • Credit contagion risks.

Although I expect that none of them have the power to derail the market, they do support a rotation to value and make clear that this is not only a stock-picking market but also a market where we may have to deploy very thorough stock picking to generate alpha and avoid risks that didn’t exist a few years ago.

Needless to say, I’ll keep you up to date!
